serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton seekh kabab consists of 2 skewers weighing approximately 100g, as commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Seekh Kabab — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Seekh Kabab. All per-100g values are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. The HPB entry reports energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol. Fatty acid fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water content are not individually reported in this HPB entry and are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton seekh kabab. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate content is 7.44g/100g, primarily from spice binders and onion. GL per 100g serving = (30 × 7.44) / 100 = 2.2, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg per 100g — approaching the upper threshold for a single dish component in a sodium-restricted diet (target <1500–2000mg/day total)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 50–75g, request reduced salt in the marinade, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salted raita or pickles; the potassium content (210mg/100g) provides only marginal compensatory benefit given the negligible dietary fibre (0.3g/100g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44g/100g), low sugar (4.69g/100g), high protein (14.05g/100g), and estimated low GI (~30) collectively support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton seekh kabab is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed without sweet chutneys or high-GI accompaniments; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response, and the estimated glycaemic load per 100g serving is approximately 2.2 (low).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58g/100g from red meat (mutton), cholesterol 85mg/100g, and sodium 467mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk markers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume mutton seekh kabab in moderation (≤100g per occasion, ≤1–2 times per week) due to the saturated fat load inherent in mutton and the elevated sodium content; grilling is preferred over frying as it reduces additional fat uptake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which directly amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA disease activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton seekh kabab is not recommended for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis; substituting mutton with chicken or fish seekh kabab would substantially reduce the arachidonic acid load, and green chili (a nightshade) should be monitored for individual RA sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Seekh Kabab is a popular South Asian grilled dish consisting of spiced minced mutton (lamb) moulded onto metal or bamboo skewers and cooked over a charcoal grill or in a tandoor oven. The minced mutton is blended with finely chopped onion, green chili, ginger, garlic, and a mixture of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala before being shaped into elongated cylinders around the skewer. The result is a firm, slightly charred exterior with a juicy, spiced interior. Seekh kabab has deep roots in Mughal cuisine and is widely consumed across India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh, and is a staple at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, 7.44g carbohydrate, 4.69g sugar, 0.3g dietary fibre, and 467mg sodium. The high protein content and low carbohydrate profile make it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, though the elevated sodium (467mg/100g) and red meat fat content require caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is typically served with mint chutney, sliced onion rings, and lemon wedges as a starter or main course at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is seekh kabab good for diabetes?
Can I eat seekh kabab with high blood pressure?
Mutton seekh kabab vs chicken seekh kabab

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a confidence score of 0.95. The food entry is an exact match to the canonical dish name. All core macros and secondary nutrients reported by HPB are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from analogous grilled meat data from the University of Sydney GI Database, reducing GI confidence to an analogous estimate but not affecting overall record confidence given the low-carbohydrate profile.
notes: Fields set to -1 and missing-data basis: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1 — the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Seekh Kabab does not report individual fatty acid fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, or trans fat) for this dish as of 2026; these values are not available in the HPB Foodo source consulted. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1 — the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry does not report a micronutrient vitamin panel for this specific mutton seekh kabab preparation as of 2026; these values are not available in the HPB Foodo source consulted. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1 — phosphorus, zinc, and selenium are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. water_g set to -1 — moisture content is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mutton seekh kabab was found in peer-reviewed literature. Halal status is inferred from the dish composition (mutton, spices, no pork or alcohol); formal halal certification would need to be verified at point of purchase. Green chili is a nightshade and may warrant monitoring for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Charcoal grilling produces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); frequency of consumption should be moderated.
